title: Using AI to outsmart drug-resistant bacteria
summary: Google DeepMind researchers at the University of Cambridge use AI tools including AlphaFold, Gemini, and Co-Scientist to identify bacterial resistance mechanisms against antibiotics. The approach compresses a multi-year discovery process into minutes, addressing the challenge of antimicrobial resistance.
writeup: This short documentary segment profiles researchers tackling the antimicrobial resistance crisis, where new antibiotics become ineffective almost as soon as they are deployed. The through-line is how AI tools, including DeepMind's structure prediction software and Gemini, have compressed years-long experimental workflows into minutes and generated hypotheses that researchers did not explicitly request. The researchers emphasize that the real power lies in AI networks detecting correlations and patterns that are not intuitively visible to the human eye.

key_points: Antimicrobial resistance is described as a silent pandemic: resistance to newly generated antimicrobial agents arises almost immediately, making the problem inherent to biological systems rather than a fixable gap.
DeepMind tools have reduced protein structure elucidation from years of experimental work to roughly 6 minutes, enabling discovery throughput that was previously impossible.
Gemini generated out-of-box ideas that researchers did not explicitly ask for, connecting dots across earlier questions and shifting researchers' perspectives on the problem space.

Neural network pattern recognition surfaces correlations not apparent to human inspection, and those matches are what allow researchers to identify new treatment strategies for life-threatening infections.

chapters.title: The Arms Race: Resistance Outpacing Discovery
